metadata.title: Law of Large Numbers — Convergence
metadata.topic: CONTEMPORARY MATH — LAW OF LARGE NUMBERS

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Watching the five simulations scatter from zero to one at n equals ten, then collapse inside one percent at a hundred thousand, made convergence tangible for me. Now I want to know the cost of detecting unfairness. Help me: (1) run the biased coin at P equals zero-point-three and find how many flips it takes to exclude fair at ninety-five percent confidence; (2) repeat for P equals zero-point-four-five, a subtler bias; (3) plot required sample size against the size of the bias.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

lines: Law of Large Numbers, taken apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Flip ten coins and you might get eight heads — that doesn't disprove fairness. Flip a hundred thousand and you'll be within one percent of fifty-fifty every time. The law of large numbers is the guarantee underneath every casino, every insurance table, every A-B test. This video runs the simulation and shows exactly when randomness loses its grip.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'Randomness vs Volume — when does noise collapse to signal?'; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to run it. Simulate coin flips using random-dot-random less-than zero-point-five, for n in ten, a hundred, a thousand, ten thousand, a hundred thousand trials. Run five independent simulations per n. Print a table: n, min empirical P, max empirical P, mean empirical P, mean deviation from zero-point-five. Repeat for die-roll P of six equals one-sixth.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: simulate coin flips using random.random()<0.5 for n in [10,100,1000,10000,100000] trials. Run 5 independent simulations per n. Print table: n, min empirical P, max empirical P, mean empirical P, mean deviation from 0.5. Repeat for die roll P(6)=1/6. No external libraries."
props.topic: CONTEMPORARY MATH — LAW OF LARGE NUMBERS

beats.narration_text: Read it. The key line: empirical underscore p equals sum of one for each trial where random-dot-random is less than zero-point-five, divided by n. No library — this is raw probability. The table will show how fast the spread collapses as n grows.

props.topic: CONTEMPORARY MATH — LAW OF LARGE NUMBERS
props.code:
# lln_sim.py  —  Claude Code output
import random

def run_sim(n, threshold=0.5):
    empirical_p = sum(1 for _ in range(n)
                      if random.random() < threshold) / n
    return empirical_p

nvals = [10, 100, 1000, 10000, 100000]
for n in nvals:
    results = [run_sim(n) for _ in range(5)]
    print(f'n={n:>7}: min={min(results):.4f}  '
          f'max={max(results):.4f}  '
          f'mean={sum(results)/5:.4f}  '
          f'dev={abs(sum(results)/5-0.5):.4f}')

beats.narration_text: Plot it. The x-axis is log scale — one, ten, a hundred, all the way to a hundred thousand. At n equals ten the simulations scatter wildly, from zero to one. At n equals a thousand they're in the five-percent band. At n equals a hundred thousand they're inside one percent of the truth. The law is visible.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: three simulation runs converging toward p=0.5 on a log-x axis; dashed theoretical line; shaded 5% band; labels showing scatter collapsing

beats.narration_text: The coin is symmetric. Try an asymmetric case — update the script: run a biased coin with P equals zero-point-three, and show how many trials are needed to distinguish it from fair with ninety-five percent confidence.

props.command: claude "update lln_sim.py: add biased coin p=0.3. For each n, print a confidence interval (mean ± 1.96*sqrt(p*(1-p)/n)). Show the first n where the CI excludes 0.5."
props.topic: CONTEMPORARY MATH — LAW OF LARGE NUMBERS

beats.role_note: slate — fill with screen recording or Manim
beats.narration_text: The confidence interval tells you when the data has spoken: around n equals one hundred trials you can reliably distinguish p equals zero-point-three from a fair coin. Below that, you can't tell noise from signal — no matter how many times you've seen it happen.
beats.visual_intent: Revised table: biased coin CI column showing first n where 0.5 is excluded from the interval

beats.narration_text: The law of large numbers doesn't promise any single trial will be fair — it promises the average will be, given enough of them. The question is always: enough is how many? The simulation gives you the exact answer for your problem.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'n=10: scatter ±40% · n=100: ±10% · n=100k: ±0.5%' — three numbers that tell the whole story

beats.narration_text: Your move: swap the coin for your own A-B test metric. What's your baseline conversion rate? What effect size matters? The same simulation tells you the minimum sample size before your test results mean anything.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) replace coin with A/B metric, 2) find minimum n for your effect size

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
